label: agent-flow after DDD refactor — clean run (no memory pollution)
source: automated — agent-flow -p --debug, memory cleared before run (rm -rf /tmp/.agent-flow/)
notes: Clean eval run post-DDD refactor. Memory cleared before all tasks. Simple tasks now take 4 turns each (agent writes files + tests even for simple prompts — behavioral change vs pre-DDD). Complex tasks produce larger, more thorough implementations. All tests auto-run and verified.
dddChanges: packages/flow/src/adapters/ layer added (corrections, render, repo-map, worktree, llm-compaction)

total.note: 4 turns each vs 1 pre-DDD — agent now writes files + tests for all prompts

C1_lruCache.qualityNotes: 383 lines. LRUCache<K,V> with sentinel-node DLL. has() (non-promoting peek), peek(), toArray() (MRU-first). Full JSDoc. 14 tests: constructor validation, get/put, eviction order, key promotion, clear, has(), peek(), toArray(), capacity=1 edge case, generics, sequential evictions.

C2_rateLimiter.qualityNotes: 233 lines. Correct lazy-refill token bucket (no background timer). RangeError validation with exact substrings. 5 tests: starts full, consume deducts, returns false when empty, reset refills, constructor/consume validation. No injectable clock (weaker testability vs post-DDD baseline).

C3_eventEmitter.qualityNotes: 244 lines. Conditional rest-tuple for void events (emit('ping') compiles without data). Set semantics dedup listeners. Snapshot-before-iterate prevents mutation bugs. listenerCount(), removeAllListeners(event?), fluent chaining. 14 tests in 5 groups.

overallQuality.basis: All 6 tasks: compile verified + tests auto-run and passed
strengths: All tests auto-run and verified during task
LRU has(), peek(), toArray() — API beyond spec
EventEmitter void events via conditional rest-tuple
383-line LRU with 14 tests is notably thorough
weaknesses: Simple tasks now 4 turns each (was 1) — unnecessary overhead for inline prompts
Rate limiter has no injectable clock (harder to test time-based behaviour)
C1 cost $0.433 is 4.6x pre-DDD for same task

vsPreDDD.verdict: Post-DDD produces more thorough code with all tests auto-verified, but at 2.7x worse cost-per-quality-point. Simple task regression (4 turns vs 1) is the most actionable fix.
